reasoning: Agostinho, Emília, and Elizabete (children of Mateus) all share patrilect (1). The table states Agostinho and Emília speak Kotiria, so (1)=Kotiria.

Each of the four labels (1)-(4) must be used once. With (1)=Kotiria and (4)=Tuyuka, and Agostinho’s statement shows his matrilect (3) is not Tariana, so (3)=Tukano. The remaining language for (2) is Tariana.
Children’s matrilect (3) is their mother Sileia’s patrilect. Agostinho says ‘I do not understand Tariana’, so (3)≠Tariana; hence (3)=Tukano.
Patrícia’s row shows she speaks Tuyuka; on the tree her patrilect is marked (4), so (4)=Tuyuka.
A is in Kotiria (Yʉ … hira). With ‘phʉkoro’=‘my mom’ and ‘…koro’ marking language for ‘speak’, it means ‘My mom speaks Tukano’ → vi.
B is Tariana (Nohá … nu-…-noka). With ‘Taliá’=Tariana and 1st-person prefix nu-, it is ‘I speak Tariana’ → ii.
C is Tukano (Yɨ’î … yee uumí). Pattern matches Aldine’s ‘My mom speaks Kotiria’; replacing Akotí with Di’î gives another language. Di’î here is Tuyuka, so ‘My mom speaks Tuyuka’ → vii.
D is Kotiria and lacks ‘phʉkoro’; final verb hiha is 1sg ‘speak’: ‘I speak Kotiria’ → i.
E is Kotiria with verb thʉ’oha ‘understand’; object ‘Tuyuka’ → ‘I understand Tuyuka’ → iv.
F is Tukano without ‘pako’ (no ‘my mom’). ‘Ye’pâ-masa’ names Tukano; verb form uúkũ is 1sg ‘speak’: ‘I speak Tukano’ → iii.
G is Tariana with Nohádoa ‘my mother’ and 3rd-person du-…-noka; ‘Panumápe’ corresponds to Kotiria → ‘My mom speaks Kotiria’ → v.
Cagüü̃cü speaks Tukano; among Tukano sentences, C says ‘My mom speaks Tuyuka,’ which fits some Tukano speaker and is not Lucas (his mom speaks Kotiria).
Mayẽ speaks Tariana; B is the Tariana sentence ‘I speak Tariana’.
Elizabete speaks Kotiria and her mother Sileia’s patrilect is Tukano, so the Kotiria sentence ‘My mom speaks Tukano’ matches her.
Fausto speaks Kotiria and his mother Patrícia speaks Tuyuka, so he can say ‘I understand Tuyuka’ (E).
Elsa is a female Kotiria speaker. The form ‘Yʉ Kotiriakoro hiha’ (with -koro) is used by females (María’s utterance), so D fits Elsa.
Lucas speaks Tukano; F is ‘I speak Tukano’.
Kaly speaks Tariana and his mother Elizabete speaks Kotiria; G is the Tariana sentence ‘My mom speaks Kotiria’.
In Kotiria, ‘my mom’ is phʉkoro; for ‘speak X’ use X+koro and verb hi-; the 3rd-person negative adds -erara (cf. thʉ’oerara). Thus: ‘My mom does not speak Tuyuka.’
A child’s matrilect is the mother’s patrilect. Saying ‘I do not understand Tuyuka’ (1sg negative thʉ’oeraha) conveys exactly that the mom does not speak Tuyuka.
Ideal marriage requires the husband’s patrilect = wife’s matrilect. Fausto’s patrilect is K, so his wife must have matrilect K; only C (female Y|K) fits and also satisfies exogamy.
Elsa is female K with matrilect Y; her husband must have patrilect Y. Among males, only B is Y|K.
Lucas is male T; wife must have matrilect T. Only E (female K|T) fits and respects exogamy.
Aldine is female T with matrilect K; her husband must have patrilect K. Only A (male K|Y) fits.
Kaly is male R; wife must have matrilect R. Only D (female Y|R) fits and respects exogamy.
An ideal marriage has the husband’s patrilect equal to the wife’s matrilect. Mateus’s patrilect is Kotiria (value (1)), so Sileia’s matrilineal language must be Kotiria.
